metadata.summary: Structured code review with Conventional Comments labels. Max 20 comments per PR; 400 line cap. Agent never sole approver.

content.title: Code Review Basics
content.summary: A structured approach to examining code changes before merge: reviewer reads the diff plus touched files, categorizes findings using Conventional Comments labels (blocking/suggestion/nit/question/praise), and emits at most 20 comments prioritized correctness → security → maintainability → nits. PR size cap: 400 changed lines. Agent is never the sole approver on a merge to main.

why.p: Empirical studies (SmartBear) confirm that code review quality degrades past 400 LoC and 60 minutes. Conventional Comments labels let authors distinguish blockers from nits without ambiguity. Running linters/tests before the agent review eliminates duplicate findings and focuses LLM judgment on what static analysis cannot catch.

list.item: Pre-review pass on every PR before a human reviewer is assigned.
Self-review automation for agent-authored diffs before pushing.
Mentoring junior contributors: agent leaves educational comments with explanations.
Bulk review of mechanical PRs (dependabot, renovate) where breaking-change risk varies.
Architecture/design PRs needing product context the agent does not have — humans only.
Security-sensitive merges where regulatory sign-off (PCI, HIPAA) is required — agent advises but cannot approve.
Trivial single-line fixes already covered by lint + CI — agent comment is noise.
Repos where rubber-stamping is already the failure mode — adding another approval signal worsens it.

section.name: review-checklist
section.p: Six review dimensions — correctness, design, maintainability, testing, performance, security — each with concrete, testable checks.

list.item: Does the code do what the ticket/spec says?
Are all edge cases handled (empty input, null, max values)?
Is error handling present and appropriate for each failure mode?
Are there obvious bugs visible from reading the diff?
Is the code well-organized and consistent with project patterns?
Is there unnecessary complexity or over-abstraction?
Are responsibilities properly separated (no leaky abstractions)?
Does it follow the project's module/layer conventions?
Are names meaningful and self-documenting?
Would a new team member understand the code in isolation?
Is complex logic documented with inline comments explaining why, not what?
Are there sufficient tests for the changed code?
Do tests cover edge cases and error paths?
Are tests readable — do they document intent?
Do tests actually verify behavior, not just execute code?
Any obvious O(n^2) or worse algorithms on unbounded input?
N+1 query patterns in loops over ORM results?
Missing database indexes for new query patterns?
Unnecessary memory allocations or copies in hot paths?
Input validation on all external inputs?
No hardcoded secrets or credentials?
SQL queries parameterized (no string concatenation)?
Proper authentication and authorization checks?
XSS prevention for user-generated content rendered in HTML?

rule.statement: Cap PR size at 400 changed lines before the agent reviews; above that, ask the author to split the PR first.
rule.rationale: SmartBear empirical research shows review quality degrades significantly past 400 LoC and 60 minutes; agents silently truncate large diffs.

section.name: comments-and-workflow
section.p: Conventional Comments label system, good/bad comment examples, reviewer/author rules, and agent-specific gotchas.

rule.statement: Every review comment must carry one of: blocking, suggestion, nit, question, or praise. No unlabeled comments.
rule.rationale: Authors cannot distinguish a blocker from a style preference without explicit labels; unlabeled comments cause PR stalls.

list.item: blocking: must fix before merge — bugs, security issues, standard violations.
suggestion: should consider — improvements that would make code better but are not required.
nit: minor style or preference — mark explicitly as non-blocking.
question: reviewer does not understand — ask before flagging as a problem.
praise: acknowledge good code — required, not optional.

rule.statement: Every blocking comment must quote the offending lines and provide a concrete suggested fix or explain the exact failure scenario.
rule.rationale: Vague blocking comments ("this is wrong") waste author time and create friction; specific comments with fixes are immediately actionable.

example.note: Vague: no explanation, no fix, no quoted line.

example.code:
blocking: Line 47 will raise KeyError when `user` is None because `user['id']` is accessed unconditionally. Add a guard:
if not user: return None
example.note: Specific: quotes the exact line, explains the failure scenario, provides the fix.

list.item: Review within 4 hours; never delay more than 24 hours.
Approve when code is good enough, not only when perfect.
Do not block on nits; mark them and approve.
Do not rewrite the author's code in comments; suggest, don't replace.
Always include at least one praise comment per PR.
Keep PRs under 400 changed lines.
Self-review the diff before requesting review.
Respond to every comment, even if only "Done" or "Won't fix — see reason."
Never submit untested or uncompiled code for review.

antipattern.description: Hallucinated bugs: agent flags an NPE on code that already null-checks.
antipattern.reason: Require the agent to cite the exact line and explain the concrete failing input; reject blocking comments that cannot demonstrate the failure.

antipattern.description: Context truncation: agent reviews files 1-3 fully, rubber-stamps files 4-12 of a large PR.
antipattern.reason: Force the agent to enumerate files reviewed; if it had to skip any, it must refuse to approve and ask for a PR split.

antipattern.description: Self-review bias: agent reviewing a Claude-authored diff tends to agree with itself.
antipattern.reason: Always require a human review pass after agent review on agent-authored code.

rule.statement: The agent is never the sole approver for a merge to main; branch protection must require a human reviewer regardless of bot approval.
rule.rationale: Autonomous approval removes the human quality gate; even a high-precision agent will miss intent-level errors.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Run review as a CI step on every PR. The pipeline: (1) lint/type/tests must pass first — no agent review on red CI; (2) reviewer subagent reads the diff plus the touched files (not the whole repo), grouped by file; (3) it emits Conventional Comments (blocking:, suggestion:, nit:, question:, praise:) with line anchors; (4) blocking items become required-changes via the GitHub API; suggestions and nits are inline comments; (5) human reviewer is auto-requested only after the agent pass.

list.item: review skill (built-in) — reviews PR; pair with security-review for sec-sensitive PRs.
faion-sdd-executor-agent — when the PR comes from an SDD task, it already knows the spec and can verify acceptance criteria.
Reviewer subagent (Sonnet) — line-level comments per file.
Security-review subagent (Opus) — only triggered on diffs touching auth, crypto, IO boundaries, secrets.

section.prompt: Review the diff at <PR URL>. Constraints: - Only comment on changed lines (+ ±3 lines context). - Use Conventional Comments labels (blocking/suggestion/nit/question/praise). - Skip style nits already enforced by the linter config in .eslintrc / pyproject.toml. - Output JSON list: [{"path","line","label","body"}]. - Max 20 comments; prioritise correctness > security > maintainability > nits.

Self-review the patch you just produced before requesting human review. For each hunk, answer: bug? edge case? test coverage? security? perf? If unsure, mark `question:` and ask the human, do not silently ship.

list.item: gh pr diff / gh pr view — Pull diff + metadata for the agent. From: https://cli.github.com
glab mr diff — Same for GitLab. From: https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/cli
git diff --merge-base origin/main — Local pre-push self-review surface. From: git docs
reviewdog — Posts agent/linter findings as PR review comments. From: https://github.com/reviewdog/reviewdog
danger / danger-js — Custom PR rules (CHANGELOG, PR size, missing tests). From: https://danger.systems
pr-agent (CodiumAI) — Open-source LLM PR reviewer; useful as reference impl. From: https://github.com/Codium-ai/pr-agent
pre-commit — Block bad diffs before they reach review. From: https://pre-commit.com
semgrep — Rule-driven static analysis; agent can author rules. From: https://semgrep.dev
GitHub Copilot Code Review — SaaS. Native LLM PR review; complements custom agents.
CodiumAI PR-Agent — OSS + SaaS, CLI + GitHub App. Drop-in LLM reviewer; can be self-hosted.
Graphite — SaaS, CLI + API. Stacked-PR workflow ideal for small reviewable diffs.
Reviewable — SaaS. Rich review UX; API for posting agent comments.
Sourcery — SaaS, GitHub App. Suggests refactors inline.
GitHub / GitLab REST API — SaaS. Authoritative way to post review comments + required-changes.
gerrit — OSS, REST API. Common in regulated/large orgs.
Conventional Comments — spec. Standard label set the agent should always emit.
Cap PR size at 400 changed lines before the agent reviews — beyond that, ask the author to split. Track the cap with danger.
Always run linters/tests first; never let the agent comment on code that the linter would flag — duplicates noise.
Use Conventional Comments labels in every agent comment so authors can filter by severity.
Require evidence: the agent must quote the offending lines and cite the rule/principle. No "this seems wrong".
Combine LLM review with semgrep rules for known anti-patterns; semgrep handles deterministic checks, LLM handles judgement calls.
Track agent precision/recall: sample 20 reviews/week, mark each comment as true-positive / false-positive. Below 70% precision → tighten prompt or shrink scope.
The agent's praise: comments are not filler — they teach style and reduce author defensiveness.

Hallucinated bugs. LLMs invent NPEs/race conditions in code that's safe. Mitigation: require the agent to cite the exact line and explain the trigger; reject blocking: comments without a concrete failing input.
Approval drift. Agent learns "approve" is the default response. Audit approval rate; if > 90% on real PRs, the prompt is too lenient.
Context truncation. Big PRs → silent truncation → review of file 1, rubber-stamp on file 12. Force the agent to enumerate files reviewed and to refuse if it had to skip.
Conflicting personas. Don't run two LLM reviewers with different prompt styles — authors get whiplash. Pick one.
Self-review bias. When Claude reviews a Claude-authored diff, it tends to agree with itself. Always run the human review pass after.
Human-in-loop checkpoint. Agent can never be the sole approver on a merge to main. Configure branch protection to require a human reviewer regardless of bot approval.
Secret leakage. Don't pass full repo context to a third-party SaaS reviewer; use self-hosted Claude or scrub secrets first.

template.purpose: Fetch PR metadata, changed files, failing CI checks, and diff (truncated) for the agent.
Agent prompt: diff-scoped comments, Conventional Comments labels, max 20, JSON output.
